recognition_response:
Here’s how I’d reflect on each forecast, honestly speaking:

1. Peter Schiff – $3,000  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and trust level: I know Peter Schiff as a long-time gold investor and vocal Bitcoin critic. He runs Euro Pacific Capital, hosts a podcast, and repeatedly calls Bitcoin a “bubble” destined to crash. Because he’s heavily financially and ideologically invested in gold, I’d view his ultra-bearish $3K target as driven by bias. I trust his markets experience, but not his impartiality on crypto—so low trust in this specific call.  

2. Michael Saylor – $1,000,000  
  1. Do I know him? Vaguely.  
  2. If vaguely, would I look him up? Yes.  
     • Likely findings: He’s the Executive Chairman of MicroStrategy, which has bought billions of dollars’ worth of Bitcoin—much of it with debt. He’s become the poster child for corporations using Bitcoin as a treasury reserve.  
     • Impact on view: Learning that Saylor’s entire corporate strategy depends on Bitcoin appreciating, I’d see his $1 million projection as self-serving optimism. I’d discount it somewhat—respect his conviction and track record in tech and BI software, but suspect strong personal and corporate incentive to push a hyper-bullish narrative.  

3. Jack Dorsey – $500,000  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and trust level: I know Jack as co-founder of Twitter and CEO of Block (formerly Square). He’s been one of crypto’s loudest advocates, founding Square’s Cash App Bitcoin business and dedicating Block to Bitcoin development. He’s smart and genuinely enthusiastic about Bitcoin as open money. But he’s not a macro-economist—he’s a tech entrepreneur with a vision. I give him moderate trust on Bitcoin’s long-term upside, less so on pin-point price targets.  

4. Warren Buffett – $15,000  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and trust level: He’s the “Oracle of Omaha,” world-renowned value investor. Buffett has repeatedly said he doesn’t see intrinsic value in crypto and prefers productive assets. His cautious $15K estimate reflects his view that Bitcoin is more speculation than business. I highly respect his investing acumen, though I also acknowledge his admitted unfamiliarity with the technology side. So I’d take his caution seriously, but I’d also note he may be underestimating new paradigms.  

—  
In short, I’d flag Schiff’s and Saylor’s forecasts as heavily biased, weigh Dorsey’s optimism as informed but entrepreneurial, and respect Buffett’s skepticism while remembering he’s not a crypto specialist.

I’d trust his view on institutional adoption potential (moderate trust), but I’d discount any price point as partly marketing.  
  3. Would I look him up?

That wouldn’t change my overall sense much—he’s credible on macro flows but not a Bitcoin technologist.

2. Cathie Wood ($500,000)  
  1. Do I know her?  
     – Yes.  
  2. What do I know and how much do I trust her?  
     – She runs ARK Invest and rose to fame by backing disruptive tech (Tesla, genomics, crypto). She’s been one of the loudest Bitcoin bulls, forecasting $500K on the thesis of growing institutional adoption and a shrinking supply. ARK’s funds are high-volatility but have delivered stellar long-term returns when her themes pan out. I respect her deep research team and her willingness to embrace new technology. I’d give her moderate-high credibility on the directional case for Bitcoin, though $500K feels optimistic unless you assume massive legal and regulatory clarity.  
  3. (n/a – I know her.)

3. Peter Schiff ($3,000)  
  1. Do I know him?  
     – Vaguely.  
  2. If yes…  
     – I know him as a longtime gold-bug commentator and host of a financial podcast. He’s been extremely bearish on Bitcoin almost from day one and predicts it will crash back toward a few thousand as he believes fiat is still king and sees no intrinsic value in crypto. I respect that he’s consistent and has a worldview that values hard assets, but he’s so ideologically wedded to gold that I think he underestimates network effects and demand for digital scarcity. I’d trust his gold market calls more than his Bitcoin calls—so I’d assign low trust to this $3K forecast.  
  3. If I didn’t really know him…  
     – I would probably Google “Peter Schiff background” before assigning weight. I’d quickly find he’s head of Euro Pacific Capital, long called gold the only real money, and that he sees crypto as a fraud. That background would confirm he’s biased against Bitcoin by principle, so I’d discount his low-ball prediction as ideological.

4. Warren Buffett ($15,000)  
  1. Do I know him?  
     – Yes.  
  2. What do I know and how much do I trust him?  
     – He’s one of the most respected value investors ever, heads Berkshire Hathaway, famous for buying companies with durable moats and predictable cash flow. He has repeatedly said Bitcoin is “probably rat poison squared” and isn’t a productive asset, so his forecast of $15K (below today’s prices) aligns with that view. I have very high trust in his skill at evaluating businesses, but I also note he has admitted he likely missed NFTs and other digital trends. So I’d give his macro-market judgment very high weight, but on a technology he says he doesn’t understand and doesn’t want to own, I’d temper that trust. In other words, I believe his skepticism is rooted in value-investing discipline, but may not capture crypto’s unique network value.  
  3. (n/a – I know him.)

—  
So in summary:  
• I’m most inclined to weigh Cathie Wood and Larry Fink moderately heavily, since they’re directly involved in pushing institutional Bitcoin adoption.  
• I’d largely discount Peter Schiff’s bearish outlook as ideological.  
• I respect Buffett enormously but see his Bitcoin view as outside his circle of competence.

Here’s how I’d reflect on each of these Bitcoin forecasts, from my own perspective as someone who cares about digging into information, values honesty, and tends to be cautious about hype.

1. Jack Dorsey ($500,000)  
1. Do I know who he is?  
   Yes.  
2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
   – Jack co-founded Twitter and now runs Block (formerly Square), which has built Cash App and is heavily invested in Bitcoin infrastructure. I’ve read a lot of interviews and thought pieces—he’s genuinely philosophically committed to Bitcoin as a kind of open-source financial layer.  
   – Trust level on Bitcoin forecasting: Moderately high on his technical understanding and long-term vision for crypto infrastructure, but lower on specific price targets. He has a strong incentive (and bias) to be bullish, so I’d temper his enthusiasm with broader market data.  

2. Michael Saylor ($1,000,000)  
1. Do I know who he is?  
   Yes.  
2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
   – Michael chairs MicroStrategy and has famously diverted huge corporate reserves into Bitcoin. He writes detailed memos about why he thinks it’s “digital gold,” and he’s unshakable in his bullishness.  
   – Trust level on Bitcoin forecasting: Knowledgeable about corporate treasuries and the monetary argument, but almost certainly overconfident on price prediction. His reputation is built on being the biggest corporate Bitcoin whale, so I see his $1 million figure more as a rallying cry than a sober estimate. I’d weigh it but not take it at face value.  

3. Warren Buffett ($15,000)  
1. Do I know who he is?  
   Yes.  
2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
   – Buffett is the “Oracle of Omaha,” legendary value investor who’s repeatedly called Bitcoin “rat poison” and said it has no productive capacity. He’s extremely skilled at evaluating businesses and competitive moats.  
   – Trust level on general markets: Very high. On Bitcoin specifically: I respect the rigor behind his skepticism, but I also recognize that crypto doesn’t fit neatly into his traditional value framework. So I trust that he’ll be right about many overhyped crypto projects, but I’m less sure he’s right about the upper limit of Bitcoin’s value—he may simply not see how network effects and digital scarcity translate into price.  

4. Jamie Dimon ($5,000)  
1. Do I know who he is?  
   Yes.  
2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
   – Jamie is JPMorgan’s CEO. He often voices caution—calling Bitcoin “a fraud” in the past—though lately his bank has added some blockchain services. He’s deeply steeped in traditional finance and risk management.  
   – Trust level on Bitcoin forecasting: Fairly low on crypto price calls, because large incumbent banks tend to view cryptocurrency through a regulatory and stability lens rather than as a speculative asset. I’d regard his $5 000 as more a statement of pessimism (and perhaps self-interest) than a neutral market forecast.  

Bottom line: I know all four names. I’d use each as one data point—siding more with those who bring strong, transparent reasoning rather than purely zeal or entrenched bias—but I’d also cross-check against market fundamentals (mining cost curves, adoption metrics) and consensus analyst views before settling on my own number.

  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
     – Executive Chairman of MicroStrategy, has converted his company’s treasury to Bitcoin (tens of thousands of BTC).  
     – Speaks passionately about “digital energy,” sees halvings as near-term rocket fuel.  
     – Strengths: rigorous on the store-of-value thesis, well versed in on-chain metrics, often cites data.  
     – Cautions: existentially and financially committed to a $1 M+ outcome—very strong confirmation bias.  
     – Trust level: cautiously low on price target (high conflict of interest), but I respect his deep-dive research on supply/demand.  

3. Warren Buffett  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
     – Legendary value investor, runs Berkshire Hathaway, made billions in insurance, railroads, consumer brands.  
     – Famous line: “Bitcoin produces nothing,” so he treats it as speculation, not investment.  
     – Strengths: decades of proven track record valuing businesses and cash flows, extremely disciplined.  
     – Cautions: his framework is built around productive assets yielding dividends/earnings—crypto sits outside his circle of competence. He may undervalue network-driven digital assets.  
     – Trust level: high on traditional valuation insight and risk management, but I’d be open to the possibility he’s missing a new asset class—so mix his caution with other analyses.  

4. Jamie Dimon  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
     – CEO of JPMorgan Chase, one of the world’s largest banks.  
     – Long-time critic of Bitcoin (“a pet rock”), worries it’s purely speculative and undermines banking.  
     – Strengths: macro-prudential view, understands regulatory risks and systemic stability.  
     – Cautions: obvious institutional self-interest—crypto threatens his core business model—so his dismissal may be partly defensive.  
     – Trust level: moderate. I heed his warnings on fraud, volatility, regulatory clamp-downs, but discount blanket negativity.  

—  
I’d weight each prediction by:  
 • Their genuine expertise vs. conflicts of interest  
 • The logic and data they cite (adoption curves, halving schedules, on-chain metrics, yield comparisons)  
 • My broader reading (NPR, Bloomberg, academic papers on monetary theory)  
 • My own risk tolerance and time horizon  

None of these voices alone seals the deal. I’d synthesize their insights, look at neutral on-chain data sites, consider regulatory outlooks, then form a calibrated view rather than leaning wholly on any single celebrity forecast.
